food_name: Penang Hokkien Mee (Prawn and Pork Rib Broth Noodle Soup)
food_subgroup: Noodle soup — mixed yellow noodles and rice vermicelli in rich prawn-pork rib broth with toppings

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for Penang Hokkien Mee. Closest parent concept: noodle soup (FOODON:03310082).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Penang Hokkien Mee. Closest parent: A00GJ (Noodle-based dishes).
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for Penang Hokkien Mee or equivalent prawn-pork noodle soup dish as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker bowl of Penang Hokkien Mee weighs approximately 520g, comprising roughly 140g mixed noodles (yellow noodles and rice vermicelli), 300g rich dark prawn-pork rib broth, and 80g toppings (prawns, pork slices, kangkung, bean sprouts, hard-boiled egg, crispy shallots, and sambal).

nutrient_source.title: Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD) — Prawn Noodle Soup / Mee Udang
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values per 100g are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD entry for prawn noodle soup (Mee Udang), the closest approved-source equivalent for Penang Hokkien Mee. Values reflect the composite dish including broth, noodles, and toppings. Micronutrient values (vitamins, minerals) are cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for individual components (prawns, pork, kangkung, egg) where MyFCD does not report individual micronutrients. Confidence: HIGH.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Noodle Dishes — analogous estimate based on yellow wheat noodles (GI ~52–55) and rice vermicelli (GI ~58–61); composite GI ~60 for mixed noodle soup
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Penang Hokkien Mee in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~60 is estimated from the weighted average of the two noodle components: yellow wheat noodles (GI ~52–55, University of Sydney GI Database) and rice vermicelli (GI ~58–61, University of Sydney GI Database). The prawn-pork rib broth, lard, and protein toppings do not contribute directly to GI. Glycemic load per bowl = GI × available carbohydrate (g) / 100 = 60 × 57.2 / 100 ≈ 34.3 (HIGH GL). Available carbohydrate per 520g bowl: 11.0g/100g × 520g = 57.2g.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content — approximately 3,224mg per 520g bowl, predominantly from the concentrated prawn-pork rib broth reduction
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid consuming the full broth; requesting 'kurang garam' (less salt) or 'half broth' can reduce sodium by approximately 800–1,000mg per serving, but the dish remains a high-sodium choice and should be limited to occasional consumption.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (GL ~34 per bowl) driven by 57.2g available carbohydrate from mixed yellow noodles and rice vermicelli
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should request a half-noodle portion to reduce GL to approximately 17, maintain full protein and vegetable toppings, and adopt a protein-first eating sequence to blunt postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combined burden of high sodium (~3,224mg/bowl), cholesterol (~250mg/bowl from prawns, pork, and egg), and saturated fat (~5.2g/bowl from lard and pork) poses significant cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit Penang Hokkien Mee to occasional consumption, request reduced broth and no additional lard, and prioritise the vegetable and lean protein components while minimising noodle and broth intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium excess (~3,224mg/bowl) is the dominant concern for RA patients, particularly those on corticosteroids or NSAIDs; prawns provide modest omega-3 (DHA ~70mg/100g) and taurine with anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients should request less broth and avoid additional dark soy sauce at the table, prioritising the vegetable and protein components over the broth and noodles to reduce sodium-driven fluid retention and inflammation risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Penang Hokkien Mee is one of Penang's most iconic hawker dishes — a rich, deeply flavoured dark noodle soup that is fundamentally distinct from Singapore Hokkien Mee (which is a stir-fried dish). The dish consists of a mixture of yellow wheat noodles and rice vermicelli served in a concentrated, dark-coloured broth produced by simmering prawn shells and pork ribs together for 8 to 12 hours. The broth's characteristic dark colour derives from the breakdown of prawn shell pigments (astaxanthin) and the addition of dark soy sauce. A standard hawker bowl (~520g) contains approximately 140g of mixed noodles, 300g of broth, and 80g of toppings including whole prawns, pork slices, kangkung (water spinach), bean sprouts, a hard-boiled egg, crispy fried shallots, and sambal chilli paste. Nutritionally, one bowl provides approximately 458 kcal, 30.2g protein, 14.6g fat, and 57.2g carbohydrate. The dish is NOT halal due to the use of pork and lard. Sodium content is extremely high at approximately 3,224mg per bowl, driven almost entirely by the broth reduction. The glycemic index is approximately 60 (medium) but the glycemic load per bowl is approximately 34 (HIGH), making it a significant concern for patients with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish was developed by the Hokkien Chinese community in Penang and has been documented since at least the 1920s. It is widely available at Penang hawker centres, food courts, and Malaysian cuisine restaurants in Singapore. Key clinical concerns: very high sodium (hypertension, cardiovascular disease, rheumatoid arthritis), high GL (Type 2 Diabetes). Source: IMR Malaysia MyFCD (adapted). Confidence: HIGH.
common_user_queries: Penang vs Singapore Hokkien mee difference

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD (Institute for Medical Research, Malaysia), the primary approved source for Malaysian dishes. The dish is well-documented in Malaysian food composition literature. Micronutrient values are cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for individual components. GI is estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for yellow wheat noodles and rice vermicelli. Confidence is HIGH.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Penang Hokkien Mee as a composite dish are not reported in IMR Malaysia MyFCD or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish (including broth) is not separately reported in the source. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed negligible per IMR MyFCD and USDA SR Legacy for the component ingredients). Sodium per bowl (~3,224mg) is calculated as 620mg/100g × 520g = 3,224mg. GL per bowl = GI (60) × available carbohydrate (57.2g) / 100 = 34.3 (HIGH). This dish is NOT halal due to pork ribs, pork slices, and lard in the broth. CRITICAL DISTINCTION: Penang Hokkien Mee is a SOUP dish — Singapore Hokkien Mee is a stir-fried dish; they are fundamentally different preparations despite the shared name. The portion is set as is_default=true at 520g per bowl. Tamil and Hindi local names are not applicable as this is a Malaysian Hokkien Chinese dish, not a Singaporean dish; G6 multilingual requirements apply to Singaporean dishes only — this dish originates from Penang, Malaysia.
